race.name: Alpe d'HuZes
race.tagline: Twenty-one hairpins. Six ascents. One day. Cycling's greatest charity climb — EUR 100 million raised and counting.

terrain_types: Paved Alpine road — Alpe d'Huez only
vitals.start_time: Riders start throughout the day — goal is to complete as many ascents as possible before the cutoff
vitals.registration: Registration via alpedhuzes.nl. Free to ride, but participants must raise minimum EUR 1,500 for KWF Dutch Cancer Foundation.
vitals.prize_purse: None — this is a charity event, not a race
vitals.aid_stations: Support at base (Bourg d'Oisans) and summit (Alpe d'Huez). Water, food, and medical support throughout the day.
vitals.cutoff_time: All ascents must be completed within the event day. Riders aim for 1-6 ascents based on ability and ambition.
distance_options: 1 ascent: 13.8 km, 1,071m climbing — achievable for most cyclists
6 ascents: 82.8 km, 6,426m climbing — the full Alpe d'HuZes

climate.description: Early June in the French Alps means warm valley starts in Bourg d'Oisans (20-30C / 68-86F) and noticeably cooler summit conditions at 1,860m (10-18C / 50-64F). Riders experience this temperature differential multiple times per day as they ascend and descend repeatedly. Generally stable early-summer weather, but Alpine thunderstorms can develop in the afternoon. The lower hairpins can be hot and exposed; the upper sections cool and occasionally windy.
challenges: Repeated temperature swings: warm valley to cool summit, 2-6 times per day
Afternoon thunderstorms possible — lightning at 1,860m is a real hazard
Sun exposure on south-facing hairpins in the lower sections
Summit wind chill on repeated descents can cause accumulated cold

terrain.surface: The D211 road up Alpe d'Huez. Well-maintained French departmental road. Smooth tarmac throughout. The 21 hairpins are numbered and named for Tour de France stage winners. Road surface is in excellent condition.
features: 21 hairpin bends — each numbered and named for a Tour de France stage winner
13.8 km at 7.9% average gradient per ascent
Sections exceeding 11%, particularly in the lower third
Descent on the same road between ascents — repeated descending on tired legs requires attention

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Not a race — a charity cycling event where participants climb Alpe d'Huez up to 6 times to raise money for cancer research. The largest sporting charity event in the Netherlands.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: community=5 for the extraordinary Dutch charity community spirit — EUR 100M+ raised. experience=5 for the profound combination of Alpe d'Huez's legendary hairpins and the emotional charity context. field_depth=1 because this is explicitly not competitive. prestige=4 for Alpe d'Huez itself — one of cycling's most iconic climbs. cultural_impact=3 for the Dutch cultural phenomenon.

logistics.explanation: Alpe d'Huez and Bourg d'Oisans are remote Alpine locations — 2+ hours from Geneva, Lyon, or Grenoble airports. The same logistical challenges as La Marmotte apply: small mountain towns with limited infrastructure, winding Alpine access roads, and accommodation that books out well in advance. The event is well-organized by the Dutch Alpe d'HuZes foundation, with support at base and summit, but the location is inherently challenging to reach.

length.explanation: The distance is deceptive. Each ascent is only 13.8 km, but riders aim for up to 6 ascents — totaling 82.8 km of climbing distance with an equal amount of descending. The total distance isn't ultra by sportive standards, but the vertical concentration is extreme. Six ascents produce 6,426m of climbing — more than La Marmotte's 5,180m — compressed into a single, repeated effort.

technicality.explanation: One road. Twenty-one hairpins. Paved throughout. The technical demands are minimal on the ascent. The descents require attention — tired legs on steep hairpins, multiple times per day, with other riders ascending on the same road. But the road itself is wide, well-maintained, and presents no technical challenges beyond basic bike handling.

elevation.explanation: If you complete all 6 ascents, you accumulate 6,426m of climbing — an extraordinary amount of vertical in a single day. Each ascent is 1,071m at an average of 7.9% with sections above 11%. Even 3 ascents (3,213m) is a serious day of climbing. The elevation score reflects the potential ceiling: six trips up Alpe d'Huez is among the most vertical any cycling event asks of participants.

climate.explanation: Early June Alpine weather is generally benign — warm valleys, cool summit, stable conditions. The climate is unlikely to be the defining challenge. The repeated ascent/descent cycle means riders experience the temperature gradient multiple times, which requires some clothing management, but it's not the extreme conditions of events held at higher altitude or in more hostile weather windows.

altitude.explanation: The summit of Alpe d'Huez sits at 1,860m — moderate altitude. On a single ascent, the altitude effect is negligible. But repeated ascents mean spending cumulative hours above 1,500m, and the sixth ascent on exhausted legs may reveal altitude effects that the first did not. Acclimatization isn't critical, but fatigue-compounded altitude is a subtle factor.

adventure.explanation: Alpe d'Huez is one of cycling's most legendary locations, but the event is a single road climbed repeatedly. There is no variety of terrain, no journey through changing landscapes, no sense of covering distance. The adventure is internal — how many times can you climb this mountain? — rather than geographic. The 21 hairpins are magnificent, but by the fourth ascent, you know every meter.

prestige.explanation: Alpe d'Huez is arguably the most famous climb in professional cycling. The 21 hairpins — each named for a Tour de France stage winner — are instantly recognizable to any cycling fan. The Alpe d'HuZes event leverages this prestige brilliantly, combining cycling's most iconic ascent with a charity mission that has raised over EUR 100 million. The prestige is derived from the climb itself and the extraordinary scale of the charitable achievement.

race_quality.explanation: This is not a race, and it's not organized like one. There is no timing, no competitive classification, no chip timing. The organization focuses on safety, medical support, and the charity mission. Feed stations at base and summit, medical personnel on the mountain, and volunteer support throughout the day are all solid. But if you're looking for race infrastructure — wave starts, timed segments, competitive structure — this is not that event.

experience.explanation: The combination of Alpe d'Huez's legendary hairpins and the emotional weight of the charity context creates one of cycling's most profound experiences. Many riders are climbing for personal reasons — honoring family members lost to cancer, celebrating survivors, channeling grief into physical effort. The atmosphere on the mountain is unlike any sporting event: equal parts suffering, celebration, and emotion. Climbing the 21 hairpins even once is a cycling bucket-list moment. Doing it six times, surrounded by 5,000 riders unified by a cause bigger than cycling, is transcendent.

community.explanation: This is where Alpe d'HuZes is unmatched. The event has become a Dutch cultural phenomenon — the largest sporting charity event in the Netherlands. Participants train together, fundraise together, and climb together. Teams wear matching jerseys bearing the names of those they're riding for. The emotional bonds formed on the mountain — strangers helping strangers up the final hairpins, tears at the summit, shared grief and shared triumph — create a community intensity that no competitive event can match.

field_depth.explanation: There is no competitive field. This is explicitly a charity event — riders are not racing each other, they are climbing for a cause. Participants range from elite cyclists completing 6 ascents to first-time riders completing 1. The field composition reflects the fundraising community, not competitive cycling. This is not a weakness — it's the point.

value.explanation: The event is free to enter, but the EUR 1,500 minimum fundraising requirement is the effective cost of participation. This money goes entirely to the KWF Dutch Cancer Foundation — it's not an entry fee. Whether this represents 'value' depends on your perspective: you're not paying for a cycling event, you're raising money for cancer research and the climb is the vehicle. The fundraising minimum is significant but purposeful.

expenses.explanation: Beyond the EUR 1,500 fundraising requirement, participants face the standard costs of an Alpine cycling trip: flights to Geneva/Lyon/Grenoble, 2+ hours of ground transfer, Alpine accommodation at resort pricing. Most Dutch participants drive or take organized bus transfers. Budget EUR 500-1,000 for travel and accommodation on top of the fundraising commitment. Tour operator packages from the Netherlands simplify logistics.

biased_opinion.summary: Alpe d'HuZes is not a cycling event that happens to raise money for charity. It is a charity event that happens to use cycling's most legendary mountain as its vehicle. The distinction matters. When 5,000 riders climb Alpe d'Huez up to six times in a single day — many wearing jerseys bearing the names of people they've lost to cancer — the physical achievement becomes secondary to the emotional one. The 21 hairpins are still the 21 hairpins, the gradients are still punishing, and six ascents produce more vertical than almost any single-day cycling event on Earth. But what elevates Alpe d'HuZes beyond any sportive or gran fondo is the why. EUR 100 million raised. The largest sporting charity event in the Netherlands. This is cycling at its most human.
strengths: Alpe d'Huez: 21 hairpins, each named for a Tour de France stage winner — cycling's most iconic climb
EUR 100 million raised for cancer research — the largest sporting charity event in the Netherlands
Community spirit unlike any competitive event: 5,000 riders united by a cause bigger than cycling
6 ascents = 6,426m of climbing — an extraordinary physical achievement wrapped in emotional purpose
weaknesses: Single climb repeated: no variety of terrain or landscape — you know every hairpin by ascent three
EUR 1,500 fundraising minimum is a significant commitment beyond the physical preparation
Remote Alpine location: 2+ hours from major airports, limited accommodation options
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The hairpins are legendary. The cause is bigger than cycling. If you've ever wanted to suffer for something that matters, this is where you do it.

final_verdict.one_liner: Twenty-one hairpins. Six ascents. EUR 100 million for cancer research. Cycling's most profound charity event.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want to climb cycling's most famous mountain for a cause that transcends sport. Yes if the Dutch charity community and the emotional atmosphere appeal to you as much as the physical challenge. Skip if you want a traditional sportive with varied terrain and competitive structure. Skip if the EUR 1,500 fundraising minimum is a barrier.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want to climb Alpe d'Huez in a traditional sportive format: try La Marmotte (which finishes atop Alpe d'Huez). If you want a charity cycling challenge in the UK: try RideLondon. If you want Alpine climbing with varied terrain: try the Etape du Tour.

logistics.airport: Geneva (GVA) — 2 hours. Lyon (LYS) — 2.5 hours. Grenoble (GNB) — 1 hour (limited flights). Most Dutch participants drive or take organized bus transfers from the Netherlands.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Bourg d'Oisans (valley base) or Alpe d'Huez (summit). Both have limited accommodation that books out well in advance for the event. Many Dutch cycling clubs and tour operators organize group accommodation and bus transfers from the Netherlands. Camping is popular among Dutch participants.
logistics.food: French Alpine cuisine in Bourg d'Oisans and Alpe d'Huez — hearty mountain fare. Feed stations at base and summit provide cycling nutrition. The post-event atmosphere includes shared meals and celebration.
logistics.packet_pickup: Event registration and briefing in Bourg d'Oisans area, day before. Many participants arrive via organized Dutch cycling club delegations.
logistics.parking: Limited parking in Bourg d'Oisans and Alpe d'Huez. Organized shuttle services operate for the event. Dutch bus transfers from the Netherlands avoid the parking problem entirely.

history.founder: Camille van Gestel and the Alpe d'HuZes foundation
history.origin_story: Founded in 2006 as a cycling charity event combining the physical challenge of climbing Alpe d'Huez with fundraising for cancer research. The event grew from a small group of Dutch cyclists into the Netherlands' largest sporting charity event, raising over EUR 100 million for the KWF Dutch Cancer Foundation. The 'Zes' in 'HuZes' is Dutch for 'six' — reflecting the goal of climbing Alpe d'Huez six times in a single day. The event has become a cultural phenomenon in the Netherlands, with participants treating it as a pilgrimage rather than a sporting challenge.
notable_moments: 2006: Inaugural edition — a small group of Dutch cyclists climb Alpe d'Huez for charity
Event grows to 5,000 riders — becomes the Netherlands' largest sporting charity event
EUR 100 million milestone in cumulative fundraising for cancer research
The event becomes a Dutch cultural phenomenon — participants train for months and fundraise aggressively
history.reputation: Not a race. Not a sportive. A pilgrimage. The most emotionally charged cycling event in the world.

course_description.character: One climb. Repeated. The 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez — 13.8 km at 7.9% average, with sections exceeding 11% — climbed up to six times in a single day. The route is simple to describe and punishing to execute. Each ascent follows the same road: the steep lower hairpins through the forest, the more open middle section, and the final push into the resort village at 1,860m. Each descent follows the same road back to Bourg d'Oisans. The physical experience changes with each repetition — ascent one is exhilarating, ascent three is grim, ascent six is transcendent. The emotional experience depends entirely on why you're climbing.

suffering_zones.label: The Lower Hairpins (1-7)
suffering_zones.desc: The steepest section of the climb. Gradients of 10-11%+ through the forested lower slopes. Hairpins 1-7 are numbered from the top, so these are the first you encounter on the ascent. The shade of the forest provides some shelter from the sun, but the gradients are relentless. On repeated ascents, these lower hairpins become the psychological barrier — the place where you must commit to another hour of climbing.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Steep: 10-11%+ average in sections, forested, shaded

suffering_zones.label: The Middle Section (8-16)
suffering_zones.desc: The climb opens up as you emerge from the forest. Gradients ease slightly to 7-8% average, but the exposure to sun and wind increases. This is the grinding section — steady effort, cumulative fatigue. On the later ascents, this is where the mental game dominates: the summit is visible but distant, and every pedal stroke must be willed.

suffering_zones.label: The Summit Push (17-21)
suffering_zones.desc: The final hairpins leading into the Alpe d'Huez resort village. The gradient eases in the final kilometers, but altitude (1,860m) and accumulated fatigue conspire to make every meter feel steep. The crowds — other riders, spectators, support crews — build in density as you approach the summit. On ascent six, the finish line at the top is one of the most emotional moments in cycling.
suffering_zones.named_section: Summit Hairpins and Alpe d'Huez Village

course_description.signature_challenge: Repetition. One ascent of Alpe d'Huez is a great cycling experience. Six ascents transform it into something else entirely — a test of physical endurance, mental fortitude, and the power of purpose. The mountain doesn't change, but you do.

guide_variables.race_elevation: Up to 21,089 feet (6 ascents of 1,071m each)
guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved — Alpe d'Huez, 21 hairpins, repeated 1-6 times
guide_variables.race_weather: Early June Alpine: warm valley (20-30C), cooler summit (10-18C)
race_challenges: Climbing Alpe d'Huez up to 6 times: 6,426m total if completing all ascents
Sustained gradients of 7.9% average with 11%+ sections
Repeated descending on tired legs between ascents
Mental challenge of recommitting to the same climb multiple times

non_negotiables.requirement: Repeated climbing sessions — 3-4 hours of sustained climbing with short recovery between efforts
non_negotiables.why: The event's structure is unique: repeated ascents of the same climb with descent recovery between. Training should mirror this with repeated long climb efforts (30-60 minutes) separated by descents, building toward 4-6 repetitions in a single session.

The fundraising minimum is a hard requirement for participation. Starting the fundraising campaign early — and engaging your personal network systematically — reduces stress as the event approaches. Many participants find the fundraising harder than the climbing.

non_negotiables.requirement: Full dress rehearsal — 4+ hours of repeated climbing with nutrition and pacing strategy
non_negotiables.why: Fueling and pacing across 6 ascents requires a tested plan. Each ascent depletes glycogen, and recovery between descents is incomplete. Caloric intake, hydration timing, and effort management across repeated identical efforts need practice.
